summary: Added `tools/dep_graph.py`, a standalone Python tool that builds object-file symbol dependency and reverse-dependency graphs from `configure.py`, built `.o` files, `nm` output, and optional `build/GALE01/report.json` metadata. Its main decompilation use is finding leaf `NonMatching` files that can potentially be converted to `Matching` without leaving other `NonMatching` files depending on them, plus identifying high-impact files whose conversion would unlock other work.
what_changed: Created a new executable script `tools/dep_graph.py` with 548 added lines and no deletions.
Added parsing of `Object(...)` declarations from `configure.py`, recognizing `Matching`, `NonMatching`, `Equivalent`, and normalizing `MatchingFor...` as `Matching`.
Added `ObjectFile` dataclass fields for path, status, defined symbols, undefined symbols, function count, and match percentage.
Added automatic `nm` tool discovery, preferring `powerpc-eabi-nm`, then `/opt/devkitpro/devkitPPC/bin/powerpc-eabi-nm`, then system `nm`.
Added object-symbol analysis using `nm -P` POSIX output with fallback parsing for non-POSIX `nm` output.
Built forward dependency graph `file -> files it depends on` and reverse dependency graph `file -> files that depend on it` by mapping undefined symbols to object files defining those symbols.
Added default leaf analysis for `NonMatching` files with no `NonMatching` reverse dependents, sorted by number of undefined symbols as a rough complexity metric.
Added `--chain` mode to report files whose conversion would make dependent `NonMatching` files become leaves.
Added `--deps FILE` and `--rdeps FILE` modes for inspecting direct dependencies and reverse dependencies for a configured source file.
Added `--cycles` mode using DFS to report dependency cycles, capped at 20 cycles by default.
Added `--all`, `--matching`, and `--limit` options for summary output, including optional matching-file inclusion and output limiting.
Loaded `build/GALE01/report.json` when present to display per-file function count and average fuzzy match percentage.
smart_moves: Used object-file symbol tables rather than source-level includes, making the graph reflect actual linker-visible dependencies between compiled units.
Separated dependency and reverse-dependency maps, enabling both leaf selection and impact analysis from the same symbol scan.
Ranked leaf candidates by undefined-symbol count, giving decomp contributors a quick heuristic for easier conversion targets.
Integrated `report.json` metadata so matching effort can be judged with both dependency position and existing match progress/function count.
Handled multiple local toolchain setups by searching for `powerpc-eabi-nm`, devkitPPC's `powerpc-eabi-nm`, and system `nm`.
Used `nm -P` first for consistent parsing while keeping a fallback path for tools that do not support POSIX output.
Normalized `MatchingFor...` to `Matching`, which avoids treating version-specific matching declarations as separate status categories.
Kept the tool standalone under `tools/`, with CLI modes focused on common contributor questions: what is safe to match, what unlocks other files, and why a file is blocked.
decomp_lessons: Dependency-aware matching work can avoid churn: before converting a `NonMatching` file to `Matching`, check whether other `NonMatching` files still depend on it.
Leaf `NonMatching` files are good candidates for independent matching work because the PR description states they can potentially be converted without breaking other `NonMatching` dependencies.
High-impact, non-leaf files may be strategically valuable if their conversion unlocks multiple dependent `NonMatching` files.
For repository-wide decomp planning, compiled object symbols can be more actionable than manually inspecting source references.
Combining symbol dependency data with `report.json` function counts and fuzzy match percentages gives a better work-prioritization view than either source alone.
Future tooling should continue to treat `configure.py` object status as the source of truth for whether a file is `Matching`, `NonMatching`, or `Equivalent`.
naming_conventions: Tool file name: `tools/dep_graph.py`, using snake_case consistent with Python tooling.
Core dataclass name: `ObjectFile`, representing one configured source/object unit.
Graph variable naming uses `deps` for dependencies and `rdeps` for reverse dependencies.
CLI option naming is short and grep-friendly: `--deps`, `--rdeps`, `--cycles`, `--chain`, `--all`, `--matching`, `--limit`.
Status strings are normalized to the configure.py terminology: `Matching`, `NonMatching`, and `Equivalent`.
Helper names describe pipeline stages directly: `parse_configure`, `load_report`, `analyze_symbols`, `build_dependency_graph`, `find_leaves`, `find_unlock_chain`, `find_cycles`.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Use `powerpc-eabi-nm` or compatible `nm` on `build/GALE01/src/.../*.o` files to extract defined and undefined symbols for dependency analysis.
Prefer `nm -P` POSIX format for stable parsing of symbol name and type, with fallback support for traditional `nm` output.
Treat symbol type `U` as undefined and types in `TtDdBbCcRrVvWwSs` as defined symbols when building object dependency maps.
Map source paths from `configure.py` to object paths by replacing the source suffix with `.o` under `build/GALE01/src`.

A `NonMatching` file with no `NonMatching` reverse dependents is a safer matching target because converting it should not remove a dependency needed by another still-NonMatching unit, according to the PR description.
Use reverse dependencies to identify conversion chains: if converting file A removes the last `NonMatching` dependent relationship for file B, B becomes a leaf candidate.
review_feedback: No human review comments were present in the provided slice.
The decomp-dev bot reported `No changes` for GALE01, consistent with this being tooling-only and not affecting the built game output.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that modify `tools/dep_graph.py` for bug fixes, performance, or output changes.
Search for contributor comments or documentation that mention using `dep_graph.py` to choose matching targets.
Check whether `OBJECT_PATTERN` remained compatible with later `configure.py` formatting changes.
Look for PRs converting files identified as leaf `NonMatching` candidates after this tool landed.
Search for later tooling that integrates dependency graph output into reports, dashboards, or CI.
